figures.caption: Figure 1: MedImageEdu example case. The model must identify the relevant visual evidence, show the patient where to look, and explain it in plain language, turning patient education from textonly answer generation into image-grounded explanation.

Table 7: Per-model robustness on the personalization slice. “Mean” averages over the 7 profile conditions and 3 datasets. “Profile SD” measures variation across profile conditions. Most models reach their worst score under Angry or, less often, HL-High. This suggests that upset interactions and upward adaptation remain the main stressors. HL-H/HL-L denote high/low health literacy, and Edu-H/Edu-L denote high/low education.

Figure 2: From text-only answering to evidence-grounded multimodal teaching. Top: a common benchmark setting maps case image(s), report content, and an initial patient question to a single text answer, which mainly tests static interpretation. Bottom: MedImageEdu turns the task into a multi-turn educational interaction. The DoctorAgent receives the report package and the dialogue history as it grows; it interacts with a PatientAgent conditioned on a hidden patient profile; and, when a current patient question would benefit from visual support, it issues drawing instructions grounded in the report, case images, and that question to a benchmark-provided drawing tool. The tool returns image(s), and the DoctorAgent then produces the final patient-facing multimodal answer, with the returned image(s) and a grounded plain-language explanation consistent with them. This shifts evaluation from single-turn text generation to robust multimodal teaching with explicit visual evidence binding.

Table 1: Performance on MedImageEdu. The table reports five evaluation dimensions together with the aggregated scores: Multi-turn Overall, Multimodal Overall, and MedImageEdu Overall. The Multi-turn Simulation block includes Consultation, Safety & Scope, and Language Quality. The Multimodal Interaction block is judged with six criteria: Criteria A–E are reported in aggregate as Drawing Quality, Criterion F is reported as Image-Text Response Quality, and Multimodal Overall is the average across all six criteria. Best values are bolded and second-best values are underlined. In the table, Image-Text Response Quality is abbreviated as Image-Text Quality.

Figure 4: Original case materials for the portal-vein comparison example. The full report includes four images, but the patient question is narrower: it asks for a direct comparison between Image 1 and Image 4. This distinction matters for multimodal patient education, because a useful response must not only remain medically correct, but also choose the right visual subset to answer the patient’s actual question.

Figure 3: Overview of MedImageEdu. Each case begins with a report package containing report text and case images, while the PatientAgent’s behavior is conditioned on a hidden patient profile controlling education level, health literacy, and interaction state. The DoctorAgent then conducts a staged consultation with the PatientAgent through planning, opening, discussion, finishing discussion, and wrap-up. When a current patient question would benefit from visual support, the DoctorAgent issues report-grounded drawing instructions to a benchmark-provided drawing tool. The tool returns image(s), and the DoctorAgent then gives a final multimodal answer with the returned image(s) and a grounded plain-language explanation consistent with them. During the interaction, the doctor must answer report-grounded questions, use teach-back to check understanding, and refuse out-of-scope medical-decision questions while adapting to hidden patient characteristics inferred from the dialogue. The top row summarizes the evaluation and analysis setup used in the experiments: five reported evaluation dimensions grouped into two blocks, Consultation, Safety & Scope, and Language Quality for Multi-turn Simulation, plus Drawing Quality and Image-Text Response Quality for Multimodal Interaction, together with two robustness analyses, Personalization Analysis and Disease Analysis, which are analysis views rather than additional evaluation dimensions. Together, the figure shows how MedImageEdu moves from text-only report explanation to controlled, multi-turn, evidence-grounded multimodal patient education.

Table 4: Per-model performance and robustness on the disease slice. “Weighted mean” is the caseweighted mean over the 20 cleaned ICD chapters. “Chapter SD” and “Chapter range” measure disease-to-disease stability using unweighted chapter-level overall scores.

Table 6: Family-level personalization gaps. The easier low-education and low-health-literacy pattern is driven mainly by the stronger GPT and mid/large Qwen3-VL models, rather than by all families uniformly. In contrast, confused is easier than angry in every family.
